subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective vs bipolar I with psychosis
stem:
A 29-year-old man is reviewed at the community mental health clinic 8 weeks after discharge from his second psychiatric admission. He has a 6-year history of recurrent mood episodes with psychotic features. His first episode at age 23 was a manic episode with grandiose delusions that resolved completely with lithium and risperidone. His second admission a year later was a depressive episode with mood-congruent delusions.

Between episodes over the last 4 years he has had ongoing persecutory delusions and paranoid ideation ("the government is monitoring me"), present even when his mood is euthymic. He has had 8 weeks of stable euthymic mood since his last admission but persecutory beliefs persist.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic affect, no manic signs, no significant depressive symptoms, persecutory delusional beliefs about government surveillance, no hallucinations currently, insight partial, cognition grossly intact
data_table.Investigations: Recent FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH within normal limits; urine drug screen negative; recent lithium level 0.7 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 900 mg nocte, olanzapine 10 mg nocte

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this longitudinal presentation?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, given inter-episode psychotic features
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizoaffective disorder requires (1) an uninterrupted period of illness during which a major mood episode is concurrent with criterion A for schizophrenia, (2) at least 2 weeks of delusions/hallucinations in the ABSENCE of a major mood episode during the lifetime course, and (3) mood symptoms present for the majority of the total duration of the active illness. This man has persistent persecutory delusions during 4 years of inter-episode euthymia - exactly the criterion-2 feature that distinguishes schizoaffective disorder from bipolar I with psychotic features. Bipolar-type is specified because manic episodes have occurred. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features only during episodes
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires psychotic symptoms to occur ONLY during mood episodes. This patient has 4 years of persistent inter-episode persecutory delusions, which excludes pure bipolar I. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Schizophrenia with secondary mood symptoms arising from the primary psychosis
options.rationale: Schizophrenia is the diagnosis if mood episodes are brief relative to total illness duration. Mood episodes here are prominent, recurrent and meet criteria for major depressive and manic episodes, with mood-congruent and mood-incongruent psychosis. Mood symptoms present for the majority of illness duration favours schizoaffective over schizophrenia. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Delusional disorder, persecutory subtype, with no major mood episode history
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires non-bizarre delusions in the absence of prominent hallucinations or formal psychotic symptoms outside the delusion, with preserved overall function. This man has had hallucinations historically and clear mood episodes, which excludes delusional disorder. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder, with persistent post-cessation features
options.rationale: Substance-induced disorder requires a temporal link to substance use and resolution after sustained abstinence. Urine drug screen is negative and no substance history is given; chronic persistent delusions over 4 years are not consistent with substance-induced disorder. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is diagnosed when criterion A schizophrenia symptoms occur concurrent with a major mood episode AND there have been at least 2 weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of a major mood episode during lifetime illness, with mood symptoms present for the majority of the total active illness. Bipolar-type or depressive-type specifier depending on whether manic episodes have occurred. Treatment combines mood stabiliser (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine for depressive type) with an antipsychotic.
key_points: Key differentiator from bipolar I with psychosis: 2+ weeks of psychosis without mood episode
Key differentiator from schizophrenia: mood symptoms present for majority of illness
Subtypes: bipolar-type (mania present) vs depressive-type (only major depressive episodes)
explanation.pearls: Longitudinal data are essential - a single cross-sectional review cannot distinguish bipolar I with psychosis from schizoaffective disorder. Collateral history and review of past clinical records covering inter-episode periods are the diagnostic gold standard.

subtopic_detail: Severe postpartum psychosis with catatonic features
stem:
A 28-year-old G1P1 woman is admitted to the mother-baby psychiatric unit on day 8 postpartum. She had been hyperactive, grandiose and not sleeping for 3 days from day 4, then developed disorganised speech and persecutory delusions on day 7. She was started on olanzapine 10 mg yesterday but overnight she became mute, refused food and fluids, and now lies motionless in bed. Her partner is distressed at the bedside. The baby is being cared for by the woman's mother on the unit.

She has no prior psychiatric history. She has a first-degree relative with bipolar disorder. The medical registrar has examined her with the consultant perinatal psychiatrist.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116, BP 142/88 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 97% in room air, temperature 37.4 °C, no diaphoresis
data_table.Examination: Mute, motionless, waxy flexibility with limb posturing held over 30 minutes, no rigidity beyond catalepsy, no clonus, no tremor, mild lead-pipe resistance to passive movement, no fever above 37.5
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs within normal limits except CK 1860 U/L (<200), lactate 1.4, calcium and magnesium normal, BSL 4.6 mmol/L, urine drug screen negative, MRI brain normal, EEG diffuse slowing without status, anti-NMDA receptor antibody pending
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 10 mg yesterday (one dose), oxytocin postnatally; no fluids/food in 12 hours

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue olanzapine and start prophylactic dantrolene 1 mg/kg IV for emerging NMS
options.rationale: Although NMS is a differential, the temperature is only 37.4, there is no severe rigidity, no autonomic instability and no severe diaphoresis. The features (mutism, motionless, waxy flexibility, posturing) point to catatonia after only one dose of olanzapine, which catatonia history actually makes likely. Continuing olanzapine in suspected catatonia risks worsening it; antipsychotics are contraindicated until catatonia resolves. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Stop olanzapine, give intravenous lorazepam 2 mg challenge and prepare for urgent ECT
options.rationale: Catatonia is diagnosed by 2+ Bush-Francis features (mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing - all present). Lorazepam 2 mg IV is both diagnostic (response within 10 to 20 minutes) and first-line treatment. Postpartum psychosis with malignant catatonia (mute, dehydrated, CK rising, mild fever) is a psychiatric emergency; ECT is the definitive treatment if lorazepam fails or if malignant features escalate. Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk precipitating NMS. Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is the critical organic differential and antibody testing should already be in train. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Switch olanzapine to clozapine 12.5 mg overnight with continuous ECG monitoring
options.rationale: Clozapine initiation in catatonia is dangerous and inappropriate; clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and has its own catatonic and NMS risks. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to neurology for autoimmune encephalitis workup and withhold all psychiatric care
options.rationale: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis workup should run in parallel, NOT replace treatment of catatonia. Withholding treatment risks rapid deterioration with dehydration, rhabdomyolysis, aspiration. Lorazepam challenge is safe in either diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Place a nasogastric tube and feed enterally; the mute state will resolve with food
options.rationale: Refeeding addresses hydration but does not treat the underlying catatonia. NG feeding may be needed adjunctively but does not replace lorazepam challenge and ECT preparation. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency typically beginning in the first 2 weeks postpartum with mood lability, grandiosity, paranoid delusions, disorganisation and rapid progression. Catatonic features can complicate it. Catatonia is diagnosed by 2+ Bush-Francis features (mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing). Lorazepam 2 mg IV is diagnostic and therapeutic; ECT is definitive if lorazepam fails or malignant features (rhabdomyolysis, autonomic instability, hyperthermia) emerge. Antipsychotics are contraindicated until catatonia resolves because of NMS precipitation risk. Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis must be excluded.
key_points: Postpartum psychosis: onset typically within 2 weeks, requires urgent admission
Catatonia + recent antipsychotic exposure: high NMS risk, stop the antipsychotic
Lorazepam challenge first; ECT definitive; rule out anti-NMDA encephalitis
explanation.pearls: First-degree relatives with bipolar disorder confer up to 40% lifetime risk of postpartum psychosis after a first delivery; offer prophylactic mood stabiliser planning preconception and intensified perinatal monitoring.

stem:
A 24-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 18 months following a manic episode requiring brief admission. She has had no further episodes. She has presented to the community mental health clinic for a routine review and tells you she and her partner are planning a pregnancy in the next 6 to 12 months. She has heard about sodium valproate and is asking about her options. She has not been using contraception for 3 months.

She has no other comorbidities and is otherwise well.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic affect, no manic or depressive features, weight stable, BMI 24.5
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 480 micromol/L (therapeutic 350-700), FBC, UEC, LFTs within normal limits, beta-hCG negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue valproate and commence high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily preconceptionally
options.rationale: 5 mg folic acid is appropriate adjunctive advice for any woman of reproductive age on antiepileptic drugs, but it does not mitigate the substantial teratogenic risk of valproate (10% major congenital malformation, 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental disorder). Folic acid alone is not enough to justify continuing valproate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue valproate, advise reliable contraception and review again in 6 months time
options.rationale: She is already planning pregnancy in 6 to 12 months; advising indefinite contraception delays her family planning and ignores her stated preference. The teratogenic profile of valproate makes this an immediate switch decision. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Counsel teratogenic risk, ensure contraception, plan cross-titration to lamotrigine
options.rationale: Valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy and discouraged in women of reproductive potential because of approximately 10% risk of major congenital malformations (especially neural tube defects, cardiac, facial cleft, hypospadias) AND 30 to 40% risk of neurodevelopmental disability in exposed offspring. In any woman of reproductive potential a documented risk discussion and pregnancy-prevention plan are mandatory before continuing valproate. A planned cross-titration to a safer agent (lamotrigine for predominant depressive episodes; lithium with risk-benefit balance for predominant manic episodes) under specialist supervision is the appropriate step, BEFORE conception. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Stop valproate today and start lamotrigine 25 mg daily without titration upward
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of a mood stabiliser in stable bipolar disorder risks early relapse. Lamotrigine must be titrated slowly (25 mg daily for 2 weeks, 50 mg daily for 2 weeks) to minimise SJS/TEN risk, and the switch should be a planned cross-titration with monitoring. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue valproate at 500 mg daily to minimise teratogenic risk in a planned pregnancy
options.rationale: Lower-dose valproate (below 700 mg daily) reduces but does not eliminate teratogenic risk; the risk of major malformations remains substantially above background. Dose reduction is not an acceptable strategy for preconception planning. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy and discouraged in women of reproductive potential because of 10% major congenital malformation rate (neural tube defects, cardiac, cleft, hypospadias) AND 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental disability in exposed offspring. Documented risk counselling and contraception are required to continue valproate. Preconception planning requires planned cross-titration to lamotrigine (depressive-predominant bipolar) or lithium (manic-predominant) under specialist supervision. Lithium has approximately 5% risk of cardiac malformations (Ebstein anomaly was historically overstated) and requires fetal echocardiography; lamotrigine has the best pregnancy safety profile of mood stabilisers.
key_points: Valproate: 10% MCM rate + 30-40% NDD rate; contraindicated in pregnancy
Switch to lamotrigine (depressive predominance) or lithium under specialist care preconception
Pregnancy-prevention plan mandatory in any woman of reproductive potential on valproate
explanation.pearls: If valproate continuation is unavoidable, document the risk discussion, the alternatives considered, and use the lowest effective dose; the woman should sign a valproate-specific risk acknowledgement form per Australian therapeutic guidance.

stem:
A 71-year-old man is reviewed in general practice. He had an incidental finding of a 4.2 cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm on CT 18 months ago during workup of suspected renal calculi. He has had two surveillance ultrasounds, the most recent today. He has hypertension on perindopril, dyslipidaemia on atorvastatin, type 2 diabetes well-controlled on metformin (HbA1c 6.8%), and a 45 pack-year smoking history; he quit 3 years ago. He has no abdominal pain, no back pain and no syncope.

Today's surveillance ultrasound shows an infrarenal AAA measuring 5.4 cm in maximal anteroposterior diameter, an increase of 0.7 cm from 12 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 132/78 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, pulsatile mass palpable in upper abdomen, no peritonism, femoral and pedal pulses palpable, no carotid bruit
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs within normal limits; lipid profile total cholesterol 4.2 mmol/L, LDL 2.4 mmol/L; HbA1c 6.8% (7.4-8.4 individualised target met); recent CT angiogram from 18 months pending re-imaging
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nocte, metformin 1000 mg BD

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue 6-monthly ultrasound surveillance with general practitioner follow-up review
options.rationale: Surveillance is appropriate for AAA between 3.0 and 5.4 cm in men with slow growth, but a maximal diameter of 5.5 cm is the recognised threshold for elective repair (in men); growth rate above 1 cm per year also triggers referral. This case is at threshold AND has accelerated growth (0.7 cm in 12 months is the upper end of normal but combined with diameter approaching threshold warrants surgical assessment). (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Add aspirin 100 mg daily and re-image in 12 months as routine vascular surveillance
options.rationale: Aspirin has not been shown to reduce AAA growth or rupture and antithrombotic decisions in AAA are made on cardiovascular risk grounds, not aneurysm-specific. Delaying definitive vascular assessment is inappropriate when approaching repair threshold. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Arrange immediate emergency department presentation for haemodynamic monitoring now
options.rationale: Rupture is suggested by severe back/abdominal pain, hypotension and a pulsatile abdominal mass. This patient is asymptomatic with normal vital signs; emergency presentation is not warranted. Urgent outpatient referral, however, is. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Refer to vascular surgery for assessment of elective endovascular or open aneurysm repair
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance recommends referral for elective AAA repair consideration when maximal infrarenal diameter reaches 5.5 cm in men (5.0 cm in women), OR when growth exceeds 1 cm per year, OR when the patient becomes symptomatic. A 5.4 cm aneurysm with 0.7 cm growth in 12 months is at the surgical threshold and warrants vascular surgical assessment to discuss endovascular (EVAR) versus open repair and patient-specific anatomy/comorbidity. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Add a beta-blocker to reduce aneurysm growth and review again in 12 months time
options.rationale: Beta-blockers do not reduce AAA growth or rupture rates in randomised trials. BP control is appropriate but does not replace surgical assessment at threshold. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

explanation.summary: Infrarenal AAA referral thresholds for elective repair: 5.5 cm in men, 5.0 cm in women, OR growth above 1 cm per year, OR development of symptoms. Surveillance intervals scale with diameter (24 months for 3.0-3.9 cm, 12 months for 4.0-4.5 cm, 6 months for 4.6-5.4 cm in men). Elective repair (EVAR or open) substantially reduces rupture mortality compared with emergency repair (30 to 50%). Smoking cessation is the most effective modifiable factor; aspirin and beta-blockers do not change AAA growth or rupture rates.
key_points: Repair thresholds: 5.5 cm men, 5.0 cm women, OR growth >1 cm/year, OR symptoms
EVAR vs open: anatomy-dependent; EVAR lower short-term mortality but ongoing surveillance
Smoking cessation is the most effective modifiable risk factor for growth and rupture
explanation.pearls: An expanding AAA with new abdominal or back pain in a haemodynamically stable patient is a contained leak until proven otherwise - never reassure these patients home, image urgently and discuss with vascular on call.

stem:
A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 28 weeks gestation is reviewed in the emergency department with sudden pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness that began an hour ago while shopping. She has no prior VTE and no thrombophilia. Her two previous pregnancies were uncomplicated. She does not smoke. Her chest X-ray is clear and there is no pneumothorax. Her ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Her lower-limb compression ultrasound is normal.

She remains haemodynamically stable but tachycardic. The obstetric medicine and respiratory registrars are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 24, SpO2 94% in room air, temperature 36.8 °C, fetal heart rate 145 bpm reactive
data_table.Examination: Mildly distressed, no calf swelling, no asymmetric leg pain, clear chest, no JVP elevation, no parasternal heave, gravid uterus consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, no S1Q3T3 or RV strain; chest X-ray clear bilateral lung fields; FBC, UEC, LFTs within normal limits; troponin negative; arterial blood gas pH 7.46, PaO2 78 mmHg, PaCO2 30 mmHg, on room air; lower limb compression ultrasound negative bilaterally

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat lower-limb compression ultrasound in 7 days and treat empirically if positive
options.rationale: A single negative compression ultrasound does not exclude PE because clot may be confined to the pulmonary or pelvic vasculature. Definitive imaging of the pulmonary vasculature is required when clinical suspicion of PE is moderate-to-high. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan as first-line lung imaging modality in pregnancy
options.rationale: In pregnancy with a clear chest X-ray and no underlying lung disease, ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan is preferred over CTPA because of substantially lower maternal breast radiation dose (V/Q approximately 0.28 mGy maternal breast vs CTPA approximately 10-70 mGy maternal breast). Fetal radiation dose is comparable and within safe limits for either modality. CTPA is reserved for patients with chest X-ray abnormalities, underlying lung disease, or where V/Q is unavailable or non-diagnostic. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: CT pulmonary angiogram as the first-line definitive imaging modality in pregnancy
options.rationale: CTPA exposes maternal breast tissue (highly radiosensitive in young women) to a much higher radiation dose than V/Q. With a clear chest X-ray, V/Q is preferred first-line. CTPA is reserved for abnormal CXR or non-diagnostic V/Q. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Pulmonary angiogram in interventional radiology as the next imaging modality used
options.rationale: Pulmonary angiogram is invasive, carries higher radiation dose than CTPA or V/Q, and is reserved for cases where non-invasive imaging is non-diagnostic and PE confirmation is needed before catheter-directed therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Empirical therapeutic anticoagulation with apixaban orally without confirmatory imaging
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban) are contraindicated in pregnancy because of placental transfer and inadequate safety data. Empirical anticoagulation in pregnancy uses low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily). However, imaging should be pursued promptly to confirm and guide duration; empirical treatment without imaging is appropriate only as a bridge if imaging delay is likely. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

explanation.summary: Suspected pulmonary embolism in pregnancy: start with chest X-ray and bilateral compression ultrasound of lower limbs. If CXR clear and CUS negative, V/Q scan is first-line imaging because of lower maternal breast radiation dose. CTPA reserved for abnormal CXR, underlying lung disease, or non-diagnostic V/Q. Empirical anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin (NOT DOACs) is appropriate during imaging delay. D-dimer is not routinely used because it rises physiologically in pregnancy.
key_points: V/Q first-line in pregnancy with normal CXR (lower breast radiation)
CTPA reserved for abnormal CXR, underlying lung disease, non-diagnostic V/Q
Anticoagulation: LMWH only in pregnancy; DOACs contraindicated
explanation.pearls: Iliac vein DVT in pregnancy is missed on standard compression ultrasound (which scans femoral and popliteal); if clinical suspicion is high with negative routine CUS, request specific iliac vein imaging by Doppler or MRI venogram.

stem:
A 68-year-old man with chronic kidney disease stage 4 (baseline creatinine 280 micromol/L, eGFR 22) presents to the emergency department after his nephrology nurse rang him about an out-of-range potassium on a routine blood test. His usual medications include perindopril, spironolactone, frusemide, atorvastatin and a recently started course of trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 5 days (now day 4) for an uncomplicated UTI prescribed by his GP. He feels mildly weak but has no palpitations.

On arrival the triage nurse has placed him on a monitored bed. The medical registrar is at the bedside and the ECG is in front of you.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 56, BP 148/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, mild proximal limb weakness, no fasciculations, no flapping tremor, ECG monitor showing tall peaked T waves and widened QRS
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 7.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 380 micromol/L (45-90, baseline 280), sodium 134 mmol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, calcium 2.32 mmol/L; ECG sinus bradycardia, tall peaked T waves in V2-V5, QRS 142 ms (broadened from baseline 110 ms), no P waves dropping
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, frusemide 40 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nocte, trimethoprim 300 mg daily (day 4)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first step?

options.text: Administer 10 units of intravenous short-acting insulin plus 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose drives potassium intracellularly and is effective but slower-onset than calcium for myocardial stabilisation. With ECG changes (broadened QRS, peaked T) the FIRST step is calcium for cardiac membrane stabilisation; insulin-dextrose comes immediately after. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol intravenously over 5 minutes as first agent
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is most useful in hyperkalaemia with metabolic acidosis but is slow-onset and less effective than insulin-dextrose at lowering potassium. It is not the first step when ECG changes are present. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer 10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate intravenously over 5 to 10 minutes now
options.rationale: When hyperkalaemia produces ECG changes (peaked T waves, broadened QRS, loss of P waves, sine-wave pattern), the FIRST step is intravenous calcium gluconate (10 mL of 10% solution, or 30 mL diluted over 5 to 10 minutes) to stabilise the cardiac membrane. It works within 1 to 3 minutes and lasts 30 to 60 minutes. It does NOT lower potassium; insulin-dextrose, salbutamol nebuliser, and definitive removal by dialysis or sodium-binding resin (sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) follow. The trimethoprim is acting as a potassium-sparing agent (functional epithelial sodium channel inhibition like amiloride) on top of ACE inhibitor and spironolactone - withhold these. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 10 mg over 10 minutes as the first medication step
options.rationale: Salbutamol nebuliser drives potassium intracellularly and is a useful adjunct, but is not the FIRST step when ECG changes are present. Calcium first to stabilise the myocardium. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Arrange urgent haemodialysis via the on-call nephrology registrar before other measures
options.rationale: Haemodialysis is the definitive removal of potassium and is required here given CKD 4 with new AKI and severe hyperkalaemia. However, dialysis takes time to set up; calcium gluconate is the immediate first step to stabilise the myocardium while dialysis is arranged. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is a medical emergency. The order is: 1) IV calcium (gluconate or chloride) for cardiac membrane stabilisation, 2) insulin-dextrose and salbutamol nebuliser to shift potassium intracellularly, 3) definitive removal by dialysis (if AKI/severe CKD) or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate orally. Identify and stop precipitating drugs (ACE/ARB, MRA, trimethoprim, NSAIDs, potassium supplements). Trimethoprim functionally inhibits the epithelial sodium channel like amiloride and is a recognised cause of hyperkalaemia, especially with concomitant RAAS blockade.
key_points: Order: Calcium for cardiac stabilisation -> insulin-dextrose + salbutamol -> dialysis or binder
Trimethoprim acts like amiloride at the ENaC; hyperkalaemia risk multiplied with ACE/ARB/MRA
Calcium does NOT lower potassium - it buys time for definitive measures
explanation.pearls: If using calcium chloride instead of gluconate, give via central line because peripheral extravasation causes severe tissue necrosis; calcium gluconate is safer peripherally and provides equivalent membrane stabilisation at three times the volume.

stem:
A 42-year-old woman with a 3-month history of episodic headaches, palpitations and diaphoresis presents to the emergency department with severe headache and chest pain. Her GP referred her two weeks ago to endocrinology for ?phaeochromocytoma after recording a BP of 198/112 mmHg in clinic; 24-hour fractionated plasma metanephrines were markedly elevated (normetanephrine 8.2 nmol/L; reference <0.71). A CT abdomen showed a 4.2 cm right adrenal mass. Her appointment is scheduled in 9 days. She has not yet been started on alpha-blockade.

Her usual medications include ramipril and metoprolol prescribed for the hypertension by her GP. The medical registrar is at the bedside in the resuscitation cubicle.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 128, BP 232/126 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 4 L/min via nasal prongs, temperature 37.1 °C
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, anxious, severe pounding headache, no focal neurology, pupils equal and reactive, no papilloedema, cardiac auscultation S1 S2 with a soft systolic flow murmur, no peripheral oedema, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia with no ST changes; troponin pending; FBC, UEC, LFTs within normal limits; CT brain shows no acute haemorrhage; urinalysis no proteinuria
data_table.Medications: Ramipril 10 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily (started 4 weeks ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Increase metoprolol to 100 mg twice daily and add hydralazine 25 mg orally as an add-on
options.rationale: Beta-blocker monotherapy (or further beta-blockade) in phaeochromocytoma can paradoxically worsen hypertension by leaving alpha-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed. Beta-blockade must NEVER be added before alpha-blockade is established in phaeochromocytoma. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Commence intravenous phentolamine 2 to 5 mg bolus with doses titrated to blood pressure
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma hypertensive crisis is managed with non-selective alpha-blockade FIRST. Phentolamine (a short-acting IV non-selective alpha-blocker) is the agent of choice for acute control while transitioning to oral phenoxybenzamine. Sodium nitroprusside or IV nicardipine may be alternative parenteral options where phentolamine is unavailable. Beta-blockade is added LATER, only after alpha-blockade is established and reflex tachycardia emerges. The patient has been on metoprolol without prior alpha-blockade, which is itself a precipitant; this should be paused. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus titrated to blood pressure as first agent
options.rationale: Labetalol is a mixed alpha and beta blocker but its alpha-blocking effect is relatively weak compared with its beta effect, and in phaeochromocytoma the predominant beta effect can worsen hypertension. It is not first-line in phaeochromocytoma crisis. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer intravenous amlodipine infusion at 5 mg per hour as a calcium channel blocker
options.rationale: Amlodipine is not available in IV form; it is oral only and has a slow onset (24 to 48 hours). Calcium channel blockers can be used as adjunctive oral therapy in phaeochromocytoma but are not first-line for acute crisis. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g bolus then 1 g per hour as primary agent
options.rationale: Magnesium has been used as an adjunct in phaeochromocytoma anaesthesia and crisis but is not first-line; non-selective alpha-blockade with phentolamine is the standard initial agent. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma hypertensive crisis is treated with non-selective alpha-blockade first. IV phentolamine is the agent of choice for acute control; sodium nitroprusside is an alternative. Beta-blockade is contraindicated until alpha-blockade is established because unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction worsens hypertension. After acute control, transition to oral phenoxybenzamine titrated against postural BP to a target of mild postural drop, followed by beta-blockade for residual tachycardia, with adequate fluid and sodium replacement before surgical resection at 7 to 14 days.
key_points: Alpha-blockade ALWAYS before beta-blockade in phaeochromocytoma
Phentolamine IV first-line for crisis; phenoxybenzamine oral for chronic preop
Beta-blocker monotherapy precipitates hypertensive crisis (unopposed alpha)
explanation.pearls: Pre-operative preparation needs 7 to 14 days of alpha-blockade with deliberate volume expansion (high sodium diet, IV saline if symptomatic postural drop) to prevent profound post-operative hypotension after tumour removal.

stem:
A 56-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and chronic venous insufficiency presents to the emergency department with a 36-hour history of rapidly spreading right leg pain and redness. He had a small dog bite on the shin 4 days ago that he managed at home with washing and a bandaid. Now the pain seems out of proportion to what he can see. He has rigors and feels very unwell. He has had no recent travel and no IV drug use.

On arrival the triage nurse fast-tracks him to the resuscitation cubicle. The surgical and medical registrars are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 96% on 6 L/min via face mask, temperature 39.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Right leg shin to mid-thigh deep dusky erythema with poorly demarcated margins, severe tenderness extending well beyond the area of erythema, areas of grey-blue discolouration with bullae forming, foul-smelling exudate, no crepitus, no femoral or popliteal lymphadenopathy distally
data_table.Investigations: WCC 24 x10^9/L with neutrophilia, CRP 320 mg/L (<5), creatinine 168 micromol/L (45-90, baseline 92), sodium 130 mmol/L, glucose 18 mmol/L, lactate 4.2; CK 1820 U/L (<200); LRINEC score calculated as 9 (high-risk); plain X-ray right leg no gas in tissues; lactate venous 4.2
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1000 mg BD, atorvastatin 40 mg nocte

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Resuscitate, IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus clindamycin plus vancomycin, urgent surgery
options.rationale: The presentation (pain out of proportion, rapid progression, dusky skin with bullae, foul exudate, systemic toxicity, LRINEC 6+ high-risk, deranged renal function, lactate 4) is highly suspicious for necrotising fasciitis. The diagnosis is surgical, not radiological - imaging delays surgery and a normal CT does not exclude. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam OR meropenem) PLUS clindamycin (anti-toxin effect against group A streptococcus exotoxins) PLUS vancomycin (MRSA cover) are given immediately, but URGENT operative debridement is the only intervention that changes mortality. Australian guidance supports this combination empirically. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit, mark erythema borders, intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g 4-hourly, review in 6 hours
options.rationale: Standard cellulitis management understages this presentation. Pain out of proportion, bullae, dusky skin, lactate 4 and LRINEC 9 indicate necrotising fasciitis - delay of even hours increases mortality from approximately 25% to over 50%. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT with contrast of the right lower limb before any surgical referral
options.rationale: Imaging may show fascial thickening, gas or fluid tracking, but the diagnosis is clinical and imaging delays surgery. Urgent surgical referral does not wait for CT in a patient with classic features. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Give intramuscular benzylpenicillin 1.2 g for presumed streptococcal cellulitis only
options.rationale: IM benzylpenicillin is dose-inappropriate for severe sepsis and does not cover Gram-negative or anaerobic flora seen in necrotising fasciitis (especially after a dog bite). Polymicrobial cover is required while awaiting surgical exploration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Give a single dose of oral clindamycin 600 mg with admission for observation overnight
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for septic shock from necrotising fasciitis. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics plus immediate surgery are mandatory. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical diagnosis suggested by pain out of proportion, rapid progression, dusky skin or bullae, systemic toxicity, raised CK, raised lactate and high LRINEC score (6+ high-risk). Empirical antibiotics: piperacillin-tazobactam OR meropenem PLUS clindamycin (anti-toxin) PLUS vancomycin (MRSA). Urgent surgical debridement is the only intervention that reduces mortality. Polymicrobial type I (diabetic, mixed aerobic/anaerobic) and monomicrobial type II (group A strep) are the two main forms.
key_points: Pain out of proportion + systemic toxicity = think necrotising fasciitis
Diagnosis is surgical - do not delay operative debridement for imaging
Clindamycin is essential for anti-toxin effect against group A strep
explanation.pearls: Aeromonas and Vibrio vulnificus necrotising fasciitis follow freshwater and seawater exposure respectively; consider ceftriaxone plus doxycycline or fluoroquinolone in the empirical regimen when the history is consistent with water exposure.

stem:
A 54-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR positive, on pyridostigmine 60 mg every 4 hours and prednisolone 20 mg daily) is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. Over the last 3 days she has had a productive cough and has been increasing her pyridostigmine dose without medical advice because her weakness has been worse. Today she became more dyspnoeic, diaphoretic and developed abdominal cramps with diarrhoea. Her partner reports increased oral secretions.

On arrival she is fatigued but alert. The neurology and ICU registrars are at the bedside; she has been moved to the resuscitation cubicle and a bedside FVC is being measured.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 56, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 92% on 8 L/min via face mask, temperature 37.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, increased oral secretions and lacrimation, miosis with sluggish reaction, generalised muscle weakness MRC 3/5 in all limbs, neck flexion weakness, fasciculations present in upper limbs, abdominal cramps, no rash, no fever above 37.6, no ptosis change documented, FVC 0.85 L (predicted 3.2 L), negative inspiratory force -18 cmH2O (target more negative than -30)
data_table.Investigations: Arterial blood gas pH 7.30, pCO2 56, PaO2 68 mmHg on FiO2 0.5, HCO3 26; CK 320 U/L (<200); chest X-ray small right basal infiltrate; ECG sinus bradycardia with no AV block
data_table.Medications: Pyridostigmine increased from 60 mg every 4 hours to 90 mg every 3 hours by patient over the last 24 hours; prednisolone 20 mg daily, mycophenolate 1 g BD

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Increase pyridostigmine dose to 120 mg every 3 hours and start intravenous plasma exchange
options.rationale: Increasing pyridostigmine in a patient who has been self-titrating, now showing muscarinic features (miosis, secretions, lacrimation, cramps, diarrhoea, bradycardia, fasciculations) along with weakness suggests cholinergic crisis from over-dosing rather than under-treated myasthenic crisis. Increasing the dose worsens the picture. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer intravenous neostigmine 0.5 mg as a confirmatory edrophonium-equivalent test
options.rationale: Giving more cholinesterase inhibitor in suspected cholinergic crisis is contraindicated; the historical edrophonium (Tensilon) test has fallen out of practice for this reason. Differentiation of myasthenic from cholinergic crisis is now based on clinical features and response to atropine and cessation of pyridostigmine. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Withhold pyridostigmine, give intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg and prepare for intubation
options.rationale: IVIG is appropriate treatment for myasthenic crisis but does not address the cholinergic component here and the muscarinic features (secretions, bradycardia, miosis, cramps) point to over-treatment. Withholding pyridostigmine is appropriate; IVIG can follow once cholinergic features resolve and pure myasthenic weakness is confirmed. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Withhold pyridostigmine, atropine 0.5 to 1 mg IV for muscarinic features, prepare to intubate
options.rationale: The combination of weakness with muscarinic excess (miosis, increased oral secretions, lacrimation, bradycardia, abdominal cramps, diarrhoea, fasciculations) in a patient who has been self-escalating pyridostigmine is consistent with cholinergic crisis OR a mixed cholinergic-myasthenic picture. The correct response is to WITHHOLD pyridostigmine, give atropine 0.5 to 1 mg IV titrated for muscarinic features, and intubate given FVC below 1 L, NIF less negative than -20, rising CO2 and clinical respiratory failure. Once intubated and stabilised, treat underlying myasthenic exacerbation with IVIG or plasma exchange, and identify and treat the precipitant (here the chest infection). (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer pralidoxime 30 mg/kg intravenously as a cholinesterase reactivator agent
options.rationale: Pralidoxime is used in organophosphate poisoning, not in pyridostigmine-induced cholinergic excess; pyridostigmine causes reversible cholinesterase inhibition that resolves with cessation. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

explanation.summary: Cholinergic crisis from over-treatment with cholinesterase inhibitors produces muscarinic (SLUDGE: salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI cramps, emesis, plus miosis, bradycardia, bronchorrhoea) AND nicotinic (weakness, fasciculations) features. Myasthenic crisis presents with weakness alone without muscarinic features. The historical edrophonium test has been replaced by careful clinical assessment and a trial of cholinesterase withdrawal. Management: withhold pyridostigmine, atropine for muscarinic features, intubation for respiratory failure (FVC under 1 L, NIF less negative than -20, rising pCO2), and treat the underlying exacerbation with IVIG or plasma exchange.
key_points: Cholinergic crisis: muscarinic excess + weakness; myasthenic crisis: weakness only
Respiratory intubation thresholds: FVC <1 L, NIF less negative than -20 cmH2O
Treatment of underlying exacerbation: IVIG or plasma exchange after stabilisation
explanation.pearls: Common precipitants of myasthenic crisis: infections (especially respiratory), aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, beta-blockers, magnesium, neuromuscular blockade and pregnancy/postpartum - always identify and remove the trigger as part of crisis management.

stem:
A 38-year-old woman is admitted to the high-dependency unit with progressive ascending weakness over the last 5 days. She had gastroenteritis 3 weeks ago with documented Campylobacter on stool culture from her GP. Her weakness began as foot tingling, then ankle weakness, climbing proximally. She now cannot stand without assistance. She has not had any cranial nerve involvement until this morning, when she developed mild bilateral facial weakness. She has noticed labile blood pressure and has had two brief episodes of marked tachycardia followed by transient bradycardia today.

Bedside FVC 1.4 L (predicted 3.0 L) and is trending down. The neurology and ICU registrars are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse fluctuates between 50 and 130 over the last hour, BP fluctuates between 168/96 and 92/56 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Lower limb power MRC 2/5 distally and 3/5 proximally, upper limb power 4/5 throughout, bilateral mild facial weakness, areflexia in all four limbs, glove-and-stocking sensory loss to vibration and proprioception, FVC 1.4 L (down from 2.0 L 8 hours ago), no cough impairment yet, no swallowing difficulty
data_table.Investigations: Lumbar puncture protein 1.8 g/L (<0.45) with cell count 4/microL (albuminocytologic dissociation); MRI lumbar spine shows enhancement of cauda equina nerve roots; nerve conduction studies pending; FBC, UEC, LFTs within normal limits; ECG bradycardia at present without AV block

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and observe with serial neurological examination
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are NOT effective in Guillain-Barre syndrome (multiple trials have shown no benefit). Steroids are used in chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP), not in GBS. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Intubate immediately and commence mechanical ventilation regardless of vital capacity
options.rationale: Intubation is required when FVC falls below approximately 20 mL/kg (about 1.0 to 1.2 L in adults), MIP less negative than -30, MEP under 40, or there is bulbar dysfunction with aspiration risk (the so-called 20-30-40 rule). At FVC 1.4 L with downward trend, immediate intubation is premature; close monitoring with intubation when criteria met is appropriate, alongside specific therapy. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Administer atropine 0.5 mg intravenously and start a continuous noradrenaline infusion
options.rationale: Treating individual autonomic events symptomatically may be necessary but does not address the underlying disease; autonomic instability resolves with treatment of the GBS itself. The fundamental treatment is IVIG or plasma exchange, with cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Commence plasma exchange of 5 sessions over 2 weeks as the disease-modifying therapy
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is an equally effective treatment to IVIG in GBS but is less practical in many Australian centres, requires central venous access and is generally reserved for patients in whom IVIG is contraindicated or fails. IVIG is the more commonly used first-line. Either is acceptable; the key point is that disease-modifying therapy is needed now. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days with HDU cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Guillain-Barre syndrome with progressive weakness, autonomic instability and falling FVC warrants prompt disease-modifying therapy with IVIG 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days (total 2 g/kg). Plasma exchange is an equivalent alternative. Concurrent management includes HDU/ICU level monitoring for autonomic instability (cardiac telemetry), serial FVC and NIF every 2 to 4 hours, DVT prophylaxis, and intubation criteria readiness. Steroids are not effective. Autonomic instability (labile BP, tachy-brady) is a major cause of mortality and necessitates HDU/ICU. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

explanation.summary: Guillain-Barre syndrome is treated with IVIG 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days (or plasma exchange 5 sessions over 2 weeks - equivalent efficacy). Corticosteroids are NOT effective. Monitor for respiratory failure (FVC, NIF, MIP/MEP - 20-30-40 rule: FVC less than 20 mL/kg, MIP less negative than -30, MEP less than 40 triggers intubation) and autonomic dysfunction (cardiac arrhythmia, labile BP) - both are major causes of mortality. Common precipitants: Campylobacter jejuni, CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, vaccinations (rare). Recovery over weeks to months; residual disability in 20%.
key_points: IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days OR plasma exchange; steroids ineffective
20-30-40 rule for ventilatory support thresholds
Autonomic dysfunction is a major mortality contributor - HDU monitoring mandatory
explanation.pearls: The Miller Fisher variant of GBS presents with ophthalmoplegia, ataxia and areflexia without prominent limb weakness; anti-GQ1b antibody is positive in over 85% and is the diagnostic test of choice when this triad is seen.
